en.content: Find the equation of the straight line which passes through the point $(1-2)$ and cuts off equal intercepts from axes.
en.explanation:
Let the intercepts along the $X$ and $Y$-axes are $a$ and $a$ respectively.

$\therefore$ Equation of the line is $$\quad \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{a}=1\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Since, the point $(1,-2)$ lies on the line,

$\therefore\quad\frac{1}{a}-\frac{2}{a}$

$\Rightarrow\quad \frac{1-2}{a}$

$\Rightarrow \quad a=-1$

On putting $a=-1$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\begin{gathered} \frac{x}{-1}+\frac{y}{-1}=1 \\ x+y=-1 \Rightarrow x+y+1=0 \end{gathered}$$

en.content: Find the equation of the line passing through the point $(5,2)$ and perpendicular to the line joining the points $(2,3)$ and $(3,-1)$.
en.explanation:
Consider the given points $A(5,2), B(2,3)$ and $C(3,-1)$.

Slope of the line passing through the points $B$ and $C, m_{B C}=\frac{-1-3}{3-2}=-4$

So, the slope of required line is $\frac{1}{4}$.

Since, the equation of a line passing the point $A(5,2)$ and having slope $\frac{1}{4}$ is $y-2=\frac{1}{4}(x-5)$.

$$\begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & 4 y-8=x-5 \\ \Rightarrow & x-4 y+3=0 \end{array}$$

en.content: Find the angle between the lines $y=(2-\sqrt{3})(x+5)$ and $y=(2+\sqrt{3})(x-7)$.
en.explanation:
and $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given lines,}\quad y & =(2-\sqrt{3})(x+5)\quad \text{.... (i)} \\ \text{Slope of this line,}\quad m_1 & =(2-\sqrt{3}) \\ \text{and}\quad y & =(2+\sqrt{3})(x-7) \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \text{Slope of this line,}\quad m_2 & =(2+\sqrt{3}) \end{aligned}$$

Let $\theta$ be the angle between lines (i) and (ii), then

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{(2-\sqrt{3})-(2+\sqrt{3})}{1+(2-\sqrt{3})(2+\sqrt{3})}\right| \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\left|\frac{-2 \sqrt{3}}{1+4-3}\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \tan \theta=\sqrt{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \tan \theta=\tan \pi / 3 \\ \therefore & \theta=\pi / 3=60 \Upsilon \end{array}$$

For obtuse angle $=\pi-\pi / 3=2 \pi / 3=120 \Upsilon$

Hence, the angle between the lines are $60^{\circ}$ or $120^{\circ}$.

en.content: Find the equation of the lines which passes through the point $(3,4)$ and cuts off intercepts from the coordinate axes such that their sum is 14 .
en.explanation:
Let the intercept along the axes be $a$ and $b$.

Given, $$a+b=14 \Rightarrow b=14-a$$

Now, the equation of line is $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1\quad \text{.... (i)}$.

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{14-a}=1$$

Since, the point $(3,4)$ lies on the line.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{3}{a}+\frac{4}{14-a}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{42-3 a+4 a}{a(14-a)}=1 \Rightarrow 42+a=14 a-a^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2-13 a+42=0 \Rightarrow a^2-7 a-6 a+42=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a(a-7)-6(a-7)=0 \Rightarrow(a-7)(a-6)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a-7=0 \text { or } a-6=0 \\ & \therefore \quad a=7 \text { or } a=6 \\ & \text { When } \\ & a=7 \text {, then } b=7 \\ & \text { When } \\ & a=6 \text {, then } b=8 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ The equation of line, when $a=7$ and $b=7$ is

$$\frac{x}{7}+\frac{y}{7}=1 \Rightarrow x+y=7$$

So, the equation of line, when $a=6$ and $b=8$ is $\frac{x}{6}+\frac{y}{8}=1$

en.content: Find the points on the line $x+y=4$ which lie at a unit distance from the line $4 x+3 y=10$
en.explanation:
Let the required point be $(h, k)$ and point $(h, k)$ lies on the line $x+y=4$

i.e., $$h+k=4\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

The distance of the point $(h, k)$ from the line $4 x+3 y=10$ is

$$\begin{aligned} \left|\frac{4 h+3 k-10}{\sqrt{16+9}}\right| & =1 \\ 4 h+3 k-10 & = \pm 5 \\ \text{Taking positive sign,}\quad 4 h+3 k & =15\quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eq. (i) $h=4-k$ put in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 4(4-k)+3 k & =15 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 16-4 k+3 k & =15 \\ \Rightarrow \quad k & =1 \end{aligned}$$

On putting $k=1$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$h+1=4 \Rightarrow h=3$$

So, the point is $(3,1)$.

Taking negative sign,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & 4 h+3 k-10=-5 \\ \Rightarrow & 4(4-k)+3 k=5 \\ \Rightarrow & 16-4 k+3 k=5 \\ \Rightarrow & -k=5-16=-11 \\ \therefore & k=11 \end{array}$$

On putting $k=11$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$h+11=4 \Rightarrow h=-7$$

Hence, the required points are $(3,1)$ and $(-7,11)$.

en.content: Show that the tangent of an angle between the lines $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ and $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=1$ is $\frac{2 a b}{a^2-b^2}$
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given equation of lines are }\\ &\begin{array}{lr} & \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \therefore & \text { Slope, } m_1=-\frac{b}{a} \\ \text { and } & \frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=1 \quad \text{... (ii)}\\ \therefore & \text { Slope, } m_2=\frac{b}{a} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \theta \text { be the angle between the given lines, then }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\left|\frac{-\frac{b}{a}-\frac{b}{a}}{1+\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)}\right| \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{\frac{-2 b}{a}}{\frac{a^2-b^2}{a^2}}\right| \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{2 a b}{a^2-b^2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Hence proved.

en.content: Find the equation of lines passing through $(1,2)$ and making angle $30^{\circ}$ with $Y$-axis.
en.explanation:
Given that, angle with $Y$-axis $=30 \Upsilon$

and angle with $X$-axis $=60 \Upsilon$

$\therefore$ Slope of the line, $m=\tan 60 \Upsilon=\sqrt{3}$

So, the equation of a line passing through $(1,2)$ and having slope $\sqrt{3}$, is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2=\sqrt{3}(x-1) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y-2=\sqrt{3} x-\sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y-\sqrt{3} x-2+\sqrt{3}=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Find the equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of $2 x+y=5$ and $x+3 y+8=0$ and parallel to the line $3 x+4 y=7$.
en.explanation:
Given equation of lines $$2 x+y =5$$ .... (i)

and $$x+3 y =-8$$ .... (ii)

From Eq. (i), $$y =5-2 x$$

Now, put the value of $y$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} x+3(5-2 x) & =-8 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x+15-6 x & =-8 \\ \Rightarrow \quad -5 x & =-23 \Rightarrow x=\frac{23}{5} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $x=\frac{23}{5}$ put in Eq. (i), we get

$$y=5-\frac{46}{5}=\frac{25-46}{5}=\frac{-21}{5}$$

Since, the required line is parallel to the line $3 x+4 y=7$. So, slope of the line is $m=\frac{-3}{4}$.

So, the equation of the line passing through the point $\left(\frac{23}{5}, \frac{-21}{5}\right)$ having slope $\frac{-3}{4}$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y+\frac{21}{5}=\frac{-3}{4}\left(x-\frac{23}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 y+\frac{84}{5}=-3 x+\frac{69}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 x+4 y=\frac{84-69}{5} \Rightarrow 3 x+4 y+\frac{15}{5}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 x+4 y+3=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: For what values of $a$ and $b$ the intercepts cut off on the coordinate axes by the line $a x+b y+8=0$ are equal in length but opposite in signs to those cut off by the line $2 x-3 y+6=0$ on the axes?
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{l} \text{Given equation of line} \quad a x+b y+8=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{\frac{-8}{a}}+\frac{y}{\frac{-8}{b}}=1 \end{array}$$

So, the intercepts are $\frac{-8}{a}$ and $\frac{-8}{b}$.

and another given equation of line is $2 x-3 y+6=0$.

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{-3}+\frac{y}{2}=1$$

So, the intercepts are -3 and 2 .<>/p>

According to the question,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{-8}{a} & =3 \text { and } \frac{-8}{b}=-2 \\ a & =-\frac{8}{3}, b=4 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If the intercept of a line between the coordinate axes is divided by the point $(-5,4)$ in the ratio $1: 2$, then find the equation of the line.
en.explanation:
Let intercept of a line are $(h, k)$.

The coordinates of $A$ and $B$ are $(h, 0)$ and $(0, k)$ respectively.

$$\begin{aligned} & -5=\frac{1 \times 0+2 \times h}{1+2} \\ \therefore \quad & -5=\frac{2 h}{3} \Rightarrow+h=-\frac{15}{2} \\ & \text { and } \\ & 4=\frac{1 \cdot k+0 \cdot 2}{1+2} \\ & k=12 \\ & A=\left(-\frac{15}{2}, 0\right) \text { and } B=(0,12) \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the equation of a line $A B$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-0 =\frac{12-0}{0+15 / 2}\left(x+\frac{15}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y =\frac{12 \cdot 2}{15}\left(x+\frac{15}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 5 y =8 x+60 \Rightarrow 8 x-5 y+60=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Find the equation of a straight line on which length of perpendicular from the origin is four units and the line makes an angle of $120^{\circ}$ with the positive direction of $X$-axis.
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Given that, } \quad O C=P=4 \text { units } \\ & \angle B A X=120 \Upsilon\\ & \text { Let } \quad \angle C O A=\alpha, \angle O C A=90 \Upsilon \\ & \because \quad \angle B A X=\angle C O A+\angle O C A \quad \text { [exterior angle property] }\\ & \Rightarrow \quad 120 \Upsilon=\alpha+90 \Upsilon \\ & \therefore \quad \alpha=30 \Upsilon \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Now, the equation of required line is }\\ &\begin{array}{l} & x \cos 30 \Upsilon+y \sin 30 \Upsilon =4 \\ \Rightarrow & x \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+y \cdot \frac{1}{2} =4 \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3} x+y =8 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Find the equation of one of the sides of an isosceles right angled triangle whose hypotenuse is given by $3 x+4 y=4$ and the opposite vertex of the hypotenuse is $(2,2)$.
en.explanation:
Let slope of line $A C$ be $m$ and slope of line $B C$ is $\frac{-3}{4}$ and let angle between line $A C$ and $B C$ be $\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m+\frac{3}{4}}{1-\frac{3 m}{4}}\right| \Rightarrow \tan 45 \Upsilon= \pm\left[\frac{m+\frac{3}{4}}{1-\frac{3 m}{4}}\right] \\ & \text { Taking positive sign, } \\ & 1=\frac{m+\frac{3}{4}}{1-\frac{3 m}{4}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad m+\frac{3}{4}=1-\frac{3 m}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad m+\frac{3 m}{4}=1-\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{7 m}{4}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{7} \end{aligned}$$

Taking negative sign,

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & 1 =-\left(\frac{m+\frac{3}{4}}{1-\frac{3 m}{4}}\right) \Rightarrow 1-\frac{3 m}{4}=-m-\frac{3}{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & m-\frac{3 m}{4} & =-1-\frac{3}{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{m}{4} & =\frac{-7}{4} \Rightarrow m=-7 \end{array}$$

$\therefore$ Equation of side $A C$ having slope $\left(\frac{1}{7}\right)$ is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & y-2 =\frac{1}{7}(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 7 y-14 =x-2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x-7 y+12 =0 \end{array}$$

and equation of side $A B$ having slope (-7) is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2 =-7(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y-2 =-7 x+14 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 7 x+y-16 =0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If the equation of the base of an equilateral triangle is $x+y=2$ and the vertex is $(2,-1)$, then find the length of the side of the triangle.
en.explanation:
Given that, equilateral $\triangle A B C$ having equation of base is $x+y=2$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle A B D, \quad \sin 60 \gamma=\frac{A D}{A B} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A D=A B \sin 60 \Upsilon=A B \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \because \quad & A D=A B \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\quad \text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, the length of perpendicular from $(2,-1)$ to the line $x+y=2$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & A D=\left|\frac{2+(-1)-2}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2}}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=A B \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \text{From Eq. (i),}\quad & A B=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: A variable line passes through a fixed point $P$. The algebraic sum of the perpendiculars drawn from the points $(2,0),(0,2)$ and $(1,1)$ on the line is zero. Find the coordinates of the point $P$.
en.explanation:
Let slope of the line be $m$ and the coordinates of fixed point $P$ are $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$.

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of line is $y-y_1=m\left(x-x_1\right)\quad \text{.... (i)}$

Since, the given points are $A(2,0), B(0,2)$ and $C(1,1)$.

Now, perpendicular distance from $A$, is

$$\frac{0-y_1-m\left(2-x_1\right)}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

Perpendicular distance from $B$, is

$$\frac{2-y_1-m\left(0-x_1\right)}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

Perpendicular distance from $C$, is

$$\frac{1-y_1-m\left(1-x_1\right)}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

Now, $\quad \frac{-y_1-2 m+m x_1+2-y_1+m x_1+1-y_1-m+m x_1}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}=0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & -3 y_1-3 m+3 m x_1+3=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -y_1-m+m x_1+1=0 \end{array}$$

Since, $(1,1)$ lies on this line. So, the point $P$ is $(1,1)$.

en.content: In what direction should a line be drawn through the point $(1,2)$, so that its point of intersection with the line $x+y=4$ is at a distance $\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$ from the given point?
en.explanation:
Let slope of the line be $m$. As, the line passes through the point $A(1,2)$.

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of line is $y-2=m(x-1)$

$$m x-y+2-m=0\quad \text{... (i)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { and }\quad & x+y-4=0 \\ & \frac{x}{(4-2+m)}=\frac{y}{2-m+4 m}=\frac{1}{1+m} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x=\frac{2+m}{1+m} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y=\frac{3 m+2}{1+m} \end{aligned}\\ &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{2+m}=\frac{y}{3 m+2}=\frac{1}{1+m} \end{aligned}$$

So, the point of intersection is $B\left(\frac{m+2}{m+1}, \frac{3 m+2}{m+1}\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \text { Now, } \quad A B^2=\left(\frac{m+2}{m+1}-1\right)^2+\left(\frac{3 m+2}{m+1}-2\right)^2 \\ & \because \quad A B=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3} \quad \text{[given]}\\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{m+2-m-1}{m+1}\right)^2+\left(\frac{3 m+2-2 m-2}{m+1}\right)^2=\frac{6}{9} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & \left(\frac{1}{m+1}\right)^2+\left(\frac{m}{m+1}\right)^2 =\frac{6}{9} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1+m^2}{(1+m)^2} =\frac{6}{9} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1+m^2}{1+m^2+2 m} =\frac{6}{9} \\ \Rightarrow & 9+9 m^2 =6+6 m^2+12 m \\ \Rightarrow & 3 m^2-12 m+3 =0 \\ \Rightarrow & m^2-4 m+1 =0 \\ \therefore & m =\frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16-4}}{2} \\ & =2 \pm \sqrt{3} \\ & =2+\sqrt{3} \text { or } 2-\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \theta& =75 \Upsilon 15^{\circ} \end{array}$$

en.content: A straight line moves so that the sum of the reciprocals of its intercepts made on axes is constant. Show that the line passes through a fixed point.
en.explanation:
Since, the intercept form of a line is $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Given that, } & \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=\text { constant } \quad =\frac{1}{k}\\ \because & \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=\frac{1}{k} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{k}{a}+\frac{k}{b}=1 \end{array}$$

So, $(k, k)$ lies on $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$.

Hence, the line passes through the fixed point.

en.content: Find the equation of the line which passes through the point $(-4,3)$ and the portion of the line intercepted between the axes is divided internally in the ratio $5: 3$ by this point.
en.explanation:
Since, the line intersects $X$ and $Y$-axes respectively at $A(x, 0)$ and $B(0, y)$.

$$\begin{aligned} -4 & =\frac{5 \times 0+3 x}{5+3} \\ \Rightarrow \quad -4 & =\frac{3 x}{8} \Rightarrow x=\frac{-32}{3} \\ \text{and}\quad 3 & =\frac{5 \cdot y+3 \cdot 0}{5+3} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3 & =\frac{5 y}{8} \Rightarrow y=\frac{24}{5} \end{aligned}$$

Since, the intercept on the $X$ and $Y$-axes respectively are $a=\frac{-32}{3}$ and $b=\frac{24}{5}$.

$\therefore$ Equation of required line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x}{-32 / 3}+\frac{y}{24 / 5}=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{-3 x}{32}+\frac{5 y}{24}=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & -9 x+20 y=96 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 9 x-20 y+96=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Find the equations of the lines through the point of intersection of the lines $x-y+1=0$ and $2 x-3 y+5=0$ and whose distance from the point $(3,2)$ is $\frac{7}{5}$.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given equation of lines}\quad x-y+1 & =0 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \text{and}\quad 2 x-3 y+5 & =0 \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \text{From Eq. (i),}\quad x & =y-1 \end{aligned}$$

Now, put the value of $x$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{array}{lr} & 2(y-1)-3 y+5=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 y-2-3 y+5=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 3-y=0 \Rightarrow y=3 \end{array}$$

$y=3$ put in Eq. (i), we get

$$x=2$$

Since, the point of intersection is $(2,3)$.

Let slope of the required line be $m$.

$\therefore$ Equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} y-3 & =m(x-2) \\ m x-y+3-2 m & =0\quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Since, the distance from $(3,2)$ to line (iii) is $\frac{7}{5}$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{7}{5}=\left|\frac{3 m-2+3-2 m}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{49}{25}=\frac{(m+1)^2}{1+m^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 49+49 m^2=25\left(m^2+2 m+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 49+49 m^2=25 m^2+50 m+25 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 24 m^2-50 m+24=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 12 m^2-25 m+12=0 \\ & \therefore \\ & m=\frac{25 \pm \sqrt{625-4 \cdot 12 \cdot 12}}{24} \\ & =\frac{25 \pm \sqrt{49}}{24}=\frac{25 \pm 7}{24}=\frac{32}{24} \text { or } \frac{18}{24}=\frac{4}{3} \text { or } \frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { First equation of a line is } \quad y-3=\frac{4}{3}(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 y-9=4 x-8 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 x-3 y+1=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { and second equation of line is } y-3=\frac{3}{4}(x-2)\\ &\begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & 4 y-12=3 x-6 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x-4 y+6=0 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If the sum of the distance of a moving point in a plane from the axes is 1, then find the locus of the point.
en.explanation:
Let the coordinates of moving point $P$ be $(x, y)$.

Given that, the sum of distances of this point in a plane from the axes is 1 .

$$\begin{array}{lr} \therefore & |x|+|y|=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \pm x \pm y=1 \\ \Rightarrow & x+y=1 \\ \Rightarrow & -x-y=1 \\ \Rightarrow & -x+y=1 \\ \Rightarrow & x-y=1 \end{array}$$

So, these equations give us locus of the point which is a square.

en.content: $P_1$ and $P_2$ are points on either of the two lines $y-\sqrt{3}|x|=2$ at a distance of 5 units from their point of intersection. Find the coordinates of the foot of perpendiculars drawn from $P_1, P_2$ on the bisector of the angle between the given lines.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given equation of lines are} \quad & y-\sqrt{3} x=2 \quad [\because x\ge0]\\ \text{and}\quad & y+\sqrt{3} x=2 \quad [\because x\le0] \end{aligned}$$

$\because\quad y=\sqrt{3} x+2\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{and}\quad y & =-\sqrt{3} x+2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3} x+2 & =-\sqrt{3} x+2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sqrt{3} x & =0 \Rightarrow x=0 \end{aligned}$$

On putting $x=0$ in Eq. (i), we get

So, the point of intersection of line (i) and (ii) is ( 0,2 ).

Here, $OC=2$

In $\Delta DEC,$ $C D =\cos 30 \Upsilon $

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{O C}{C D} & =2 \\ C D & =5 \cos 30 \Upsilon \\ & =5 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ O D & =O C+C D=2+5 \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

So, the coordinates of the foot of perpendiculars are $\left(0,2+\frac{5 \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$.

en.content: If $p$ is the length of perpendicular from the origin on the line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ and $a^2, p^2$ and $b^2$ are in AP, the show that $a^4+b^4=0$.
en.explanation:
Given equation of line is,

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Perpendicular length from the origin on the line (i) is given by $p$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{i.e.,}\quad & p=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}}}=\frac{a b}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}} \\ \therefore \quad & p^2=\frac{a^2 b^2}{a^2+b^2} \end{aligned}$$

Given that, $a^2, p^2$ and $b^2$ are in AP.

$$ \begin{array}{lr} \therefore & 2 p^2=a^2+b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2 a^2 b^2}{a^2+b^2}=a^2+b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 a^2 b^2=\left(a^2+b^2\right)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 a^2+b^2=a^4+b^4+2 a^2 b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & a^4+b^4=0 \end{array}$$

en.content: A line cutting off intercept -3 from the $Y$-axis and the tangent at angle to the $X$-axis is $\frac{3}{5}$, its equation is
en.explanation:
Given that, $c=-3$ and $m=\frac{3}{5}$

$\therefore$ Equation of the line is $y=m x+c$

$$\begin{aligned} & \qquad y=\frac{3}{5} x-3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 y=3 x-15 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 y-3 x+15=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Slope of a line which cuts off intercepts of equal lengths on the axes is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let equation of line be}\quad \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{a} & =1 \\ \Rightarrow\quad x+y & =a \\ \Rightarrow\quad y & =-x+a\\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \text { Required slope }=-1$$

en.content: The equation of the straight line passing through the point $(3,2)$ and perpendicular to the line $y=x$ is
en.explanation:
Since, line passes through the point $(3,2)$ and perpendicular to the line $y=x$.

$\because$ Slope $(m)=-1 \quad[$ since, line is perpendicular to the line $y=x]$

$\therefore$ Equation of line which passes through $(3,2)$ is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & y-2=-1(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & y-2=-x+3 \\ \Rightarrow & x+y=5 \end{array}$$

en.content: The equation of the line passing through the point $(1,2)$ and perpendicular to the line $x+y+1=0$ is
en.explanation:
Given point is $(1,2)$ and slope of the required line is 1 .

$$\begin{aligned} & \because x+y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-x-1 \Rightarrow m_1=-11 \\ & \therefore \text { slope of the line }=\frac{-1}{-1}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Equation of required line is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2 =1(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y-2 =x-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y-x-1 =0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The tangent of angle between the lines whose intercepts on the axes are $a,-b$ and $b,-a$ respectively, is
en.explanation:
Since, intercepts on the axes are $a,-b$ then equation of the line is $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=1$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{y}{b}=\frac{x}{a}-1 \\ \Rightarrow & y=\frac{b x}{a}-b \end{array}$$

So, the lope of this line i.e., $m_1=\frac{b}{a}$.

Also, for intercepts on the axes as $b$ and $-a$, then equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{x}{b}-\frac{y}{a} & =1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{y}{a} & =\frac{x}{b}-1 \Rightarrow y=\frac{a}{b} x-a \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { and slope of this line i.e., } m_2=\frac{a}{b}\\ &\therefore \quad \tan \theta=\frac{\frac{b}{a}-\frac{a}{b}}{1+\frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{b}{a}}=\frac{\frac{b^2-a^2}{a b}}{2}=\frac{b^2-a^2}{2 a b} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If the line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ passes through the points $(2,-3)$ and $(4,-5)$, then $(a, b)$ is
en.explanation:
Given, line is $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$

Since, the points $(2,-3)$ and $(4,-5)$ lies on this line.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{2}{a}-\frac{3}{b}=1 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \text { and } & \frac{4}{a}-\frac{5}{b}=1\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{array}$$

On multiplying by 2 in Eq. (ii) and then subtracting Eq. (iii) from Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & -\frac{6}{b}+\frac{5}{b} =1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{-1}{b} =1 \\ \therefore \quad & b =-1 \end{aligned}$$

On putting $b=-1$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{2}{a}+3 =1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{2}{a} =-2 \Rightarrow a=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & (a, b) =(-1,-1) \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The distance of the point of intersection of the lines $2 x-3 y+5=0$ and $3 x+4 y=0$ from the line $5 x-2 y=0$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{l} \text{Given equation of lines}\quad 2 x-3 y+5=0 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text{and}\quad 3 x+4 y=0\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{array}$$

From Eq. (ii), put the value of $x=\frac{-4 y}{3}$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\begin{array}{l} & 2\left(\frac{-4 y}{3}\right)-3 y+5 =0 \\ \Rightarrow & -8 y-9 y+15 =0 \\ \Rightarrow & y =\frac{15}{17} \end{array}$$

From Eq. (ii), $$3 x+4 \cdot \frac{15}{17}=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{-60}{17 \cdot 3}=\frac{-20}{17}$$

So, the point of intersection is $\left(\frac{-20}{17}, \frac{15}{17}\right)$.

$\therefore$ Required distance from the line $5 x-2 y=0$ is,

$$\begin{aligned} & \qquad d=\frac{\left|-5 \times \frac{20}{17}-2\left(\frac{15}{17}\right)\right|}{\sqrt{25+4}}=\frac{\left|\frac{-100}{17}-\frac{30}{17}\right|}{\sqrt{29}}=\frac{130}{17 \sqrt{29}} \\ & {\left[\because \text { distance of a point } p\left(x_1, y_1\right) \text { from the line } a x+b y+c=0 \text { is } d=\frac{\left|a x_1+b y_1+c\right|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}\right]} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The equation of the lines which pass through the point $(3,-2)$ and are inclined at $60^{\circ}$ to the line $\sqrt{3} x+y=1$ is
en.explanation:
So, the given point $A$ is $(3,-2)$.

So, the equation of line $\sqrt{3} x+y=1$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad y=-\sqrt{3} x+1 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Slope, } m_1=-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

Let slope of the required line be $m_2$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \tan \theta=\left|\frac{-\sqrt{3}-m_2}{1-\sqrt{3} m_2}\right| \\ & {\left[\because \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right|\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \tan 60 \Upsilon= \pm\left(\frac{-\sqrt{3}-m_2}{1-\sqrt{3} m_2}\right) \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3}=\left(\frac{-\sqrt{3}-m_2}{1-\sqrt{3} m_2}\right) \quad \text { [taking positive sign] } \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3}-3 m_2=-\sqrt{3}-m_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sqrt{3}=2 m_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad m_2=\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \text { Equation of line passing through }(3,-2) \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} y+2 & =\sqrt{3}(x-3) \\ y-\sqrt{3} x+2+3 \sqrt{3} & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3} x-y-2-3 \sqrt{3} & =0\quad \text{... (i)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

[taking negative sign from Eq. (i)]

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{3}-3 m_2=\sqrt{3}+m_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad m_2=0 \\ & \therefore \text { The equation of line is } y+2=0(x-3) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y+2=0\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { So, the required equation of lines are } \sqrt{3} x-y-2-3 \sqrt{3}=0 \text { and } y+2=0 \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The equations of the lines passing through the point $(1,0)$ and at a distance $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ from the origin, are
en.explanation:
Let slope of the line be $m$.

$\because$ Equation of line passing through $(1,0)$ is

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & y-0=m(x-1) \\ & y-m x+m=0\quad \text{... (i)} \end{array}$$

Since, the distance from origin is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

Then, $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{0-0+m}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} =\frac{m}{\sqrt{1+m^2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{3}{4} =\frac{m^2}{1+m^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 3+3 m^2 =4 m^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & m^2 =3 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & m = \pm \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

So, the first equation of line is

$$\begin{array}{r} &y =\sqrt{3}(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3} x-y-\sqrt{3}=0 \end{array}$$

and the second equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & y=-\sqrt{3}(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \sqrt{3} x+y-\sqrt{3}=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The distance between the lines $y=m x+c_1$ and $y=m x+c_2$ is
en.explanation:
Given, equation of the lines are

$$\begin{aligned} & y=m x+c_1 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text{and}\quad & y=m x+c_2\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Distance between them is given by

$$d=\frac{\left|c_1-c_2\right|}{\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

en.content: The coordinates of the foot of perpendiculars from the point $(2,3)$ on the line $y=3 x+4$ is given by
en.explanation:
Given, equation of the line is

$$y=3 x+4\quad \text{... (i)}$$

$\therefore$ Slope of this line, $m_1=3$

So, the slope of line $O P$ is $-\frac{1}{3}\quad [\because OP\bot AB]$.

$\therefore$ Equation of line $O P$ is

$$\begin{array}{l} & y-3 =-\frac{1}{3}(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow & 3 y-9 =-x+2 \\ \Rightarrow & x+3 y-11 =0\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{array}$$

Using the value of $y$ from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & x+3(3 x+4)-11=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x+9 x+12-11=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 10 x+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{10} \end{array}$$

Put $x=\frac{-1}{10}$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$y=\frac{-3}{10}+4=\frac{-3+40}{10}=\frac{37}{10}$$

So, the foot of perpendicular is $\left(-\frac{1}{10}, \frac{37}{10}\right)$.

en.content: If the coordinates of the middle point of the portion of a line intercepted between the coordinate axes is $(3,2)$, then the equation of the line will be
en.explanation:
Since, the coordinates of the middle point are $\mathrm{P}(3,2)$.

$\therefore \quad 3=\frac{1 \cdot 0+1 \cdot a}{1+1}$

$\Rightarrow \quad 3=\frac{a}{2} \Rightarrow a=6$

Similarly, $$b=4$$

$\therefore$ Equation of the line is $\frac{x}{6}+\frac{y}{4}=1$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x+3 y=12$$

en.content: Equation of the line passing through $(1,2)$ and parallel to the line $y=3 x-1$ is
en.explanation:
Since, the line passes through $(1,2)$ and parallel to the line $y=3 x-1$.

So, slope of the required line $m=3$. [ $\because$ slope of $y=3 x-1$ is 3 ]

Hence, the equation of line is

$$y-2=3(x-1)$$

en.content: Equations of diagonals of the square formed by the lines $x=0, y=0, x=1$ and $y=1$ are
en.explanation:
Equation of $O B$ is

$$\begin{aligned} y-0 & =\frac{1-0}{1-0}(x-0) \\ \Rightarrow \quad y & =x \end{aligned}$$

and equation of $A C$ is

$$\begin{array}{l} & y-0 =\frac{1-0}{0-1}(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow & y =-x+1 \\ \Rightarrow & x+y-1 =0 \end{array}$$

en.content: For specifying a straight line, how many geometrical parameters should be known?
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Equation of straight lines are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \qquad \begin{aligned} & y=m x+c, \text { parameter }=2 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1, \text { parameter }=2 \quad \text{... (ii)}\\ & y-y_1=m\left(x-x_1\right), \text { parameter }=2 \quad \text{... (iii)}\\ \text{and}\quad & x \cos w+y \sin w=p, \text { parameter }=2 \quad \text{... (iv)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

It is clear that from Eqs. (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), for specifying a straight line clearly two parameters should be known.

en.content:
The point $(4,1)$ undergoes the following two successive transformations

(i) Reflection about the line $y=x$

(ii) Translation through a distance 2 units along the positive $X$-axis.

Then, the final coordinates of the point are
en.explanation:
Let the reflection of $A(4,1)$ in $y=x$ is $B(h, k)$.

Now, mid-point of $A B$ is $\left(\frac{4+h}{2}, \frac{1+k}{2}\right)$ which lies on $y=x$.

i.e., $\quad \frac{4+h}{2}=\frac{1+k}{2} \Rightarrow h-k=-3\quad \text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { So, the slope of line } y=x \text { is } 1 \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { Slope of } A B=\frac{h-4}{k-1} \\ \Rightarrow & 1 \cdot\left(\frac{h-4}{k-1}\right)=-1 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & h-4=1-k \\ \Rightarrow & h+k=5 \quad \text{... (ii)}\\ \text { and } & h-k=-3 \\ & 2 h=2 \Rightarrow h=1 \end{array}$$

On putting $h=1$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$k=4$$

So, the point is $(1,4)$.

Hence, after translation the point is $(1+2,4)$ or $(3,4)$.

en.content: A point equidistant from the lines $4 x+3 y+10=0,5 x-12 y+26=0$ and $7 x+24 y-50=0$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { The given equation of lines are }\\ &\begin{array}{lr} & 4 x+3 y+10=0 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \Rightarrow & 5 x-12 y+26=0 \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \Rightarrow & 7 x+24 y-50=0 \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

Let the point $(h, k)$ which is equidistant from these lines.

Distance from line (i) $=\frac{|4 h+3 k+10|}{\sqrt{16+9}}$

Distance from line (ii) $=\frac{|5 h-12 k+26|}{\sqrt{25+144}}$

Distance from the line (iii) $=\frac{|7 h+24 k-50|}{\sqrt{7^2+24^2}}$

So, the point ( $h, k$ ) is equidistant from lines (i), (ii) and (iii).

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{4 h+3 k+10}{\sqrt{16+9}}=\frac{5 h-12 k+26}{\sqrt{25+144}}=\frac{7 h+24 k-50}{\sqrt{49+576}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{|4 h+3 k+10|}{5}=\frac{|5 h-12 k+26|}{13}=\frac{|7 h+24 k-50|}{25} \end{array}$$

Clearly, if $h=0, k=0$, then $\frac{10}{5}=\frac{26}{13}=\frac{50}{25}=2$

Hence, the required point is $(0,0)$.

en.content: A line passes through $(2,2)$ and is perpendicular to the line $3 x+y=3$. Its $y$-intercept is
en.explanation:
Given line is $y=3-3 x$.

Then, slope of the required line $=\frac{1}{3}$

$\because \quad$ Equation of the required line is

$$y-2=\frac{1}{3}(x-2)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2 =\frac{1}{3}(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 3 y-6 =x-2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x-3 y+4 =0 \\ \text { For } y \text {-intercept, } \quad & \text { put } =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 0-3 y+4 =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y =\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The ratio in which the line $3 x+4 y+2=0$ divides the distance between the lines $3 x+4 y+5=0$ and $3 x+4 y-5=0$ is
en.explanation:
Let point $A\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ lies on the line $3 x+4 y+5=0$, then $3 x_1+4 y_1+5=0$

Now, perpendicular distance from $A$ to the line

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 x+4 y+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left|3 x_1+4 y_1+2\right|}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{|-5-2|}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{-7}{5} \end{aligned}$$

Let point $B\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ lies on the line $3 x+4 y-5=0$ i.e., $3 x_2+4 y_2-5=0$.

Now, perpendicular distance from $B$ to the line $3 x+4 y+2=0$,

$$\frac{\left|3 x_2+4 y_2+2\right|}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{|+5-2|}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{3}{5}$$

Hence, the required ratio is $\frac{3}{5}: \frac{7}{5}$ i.e., $3: 7$.

en.content: One vertex of the equilateral triangle with centroid at the origin and one side as $x+y-2=0$ is
en.explanation:
Let $A B C$ be the equilateral triangle with vertex $A(h, k)$.

Let the coordinates of $D$ are $(\alpha, \beta)$.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We know that, } 2: 1 \text { from the vertex } A \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 0=\frac{2 \alpha+h}{3} \text { and } 0=\frac{2 \beta+k}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \alpha=-h \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text { and } & 2 \beta=-k \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Also, } D(\alpha, \beta) \text { lies on the line } x+y-2=0. \\ & \therefore \quad \alpha+\beta-2=0 \quad \text{... (ii)}\\ & A D \perp B C \end{aligned}$$

Since, the slope of line $B C$ i.e., $m_{B C}=-1$

and slope of the line $A G$ i.e., $m_{A G}=\frac{k-0}{h-0}=\frac{k}{h}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad(-1) \cdot\left(\frac{k}{h}\right)=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad h=k\quad\text{... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { From Eqs. (i) and (iii), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 \alpha=-h \text { and } 2 \beta=-h \\ & \therefore \quad \alpha=\beta \\ & \text { From Eq. (ii), } \\ & 2 \alpha-2=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=1 \\ & \text { If } \alpha=1 \text {, then } \beta=1 \\ & \text { From Eq. (i), } h=-2, k=-2 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { So, the vertex } A \text { is }(-2,-2) \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $a, b$ and $c$ are in AP, then the straight lines $a x+b y+c=0$ will always pass through .............. .
en.explanation:
Given line is $$a x+b y+c=0\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Since, $a, b$ and $c$ are in AP, then

$$\begin{aligned} b & =\frac{a+c}{2} \\ a-2 b+c & =0\quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing Eqs.(i) and (ii), we get

$$x=1, y=2\quad \text{[using value of b in Eq. (i)]}$$

So, $(1,-2)$ lies on the line.

en.content: The line which cuts off equal intercept from the axes and pass through the point $(1,-2)$ is ............ .
en.explanation:
Let equation of line is

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{a}=1\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Since, this line passes through $(1,-2)$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{a}-\frac{2}{a}=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 1-2=a \Rightarrow a=-1 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Required equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} -x-y & =1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x+y+1 & =0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Equation of the line through thes point $(3,2)$ and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the line $x-2 y=3$ are ........... .
en.explanation:
Since, the given point $P(3,2)$ and line is $x-2 y=3$

Slope of this line is $m_1=\frac{1}{2}$

Let the slope of the required line is $m$.

Then,

$$\begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\left|\frac{m-\frac{1}{2}}{1+\frac{1}{2} m}\right| \\ 1 & = \pm\left(\frac{m-\frac{1}{2}}{1+\frac{m}{2}}\right) \quad [\because \tan45^\circ=1] \text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Taking positive sign, $$1+\frac{m}{2}=m-\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & m-\frac{m}{2}=1+\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{m}{2}=\frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow m=3 \end{array}\\ &\text { Taking negative sign, } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} 1 & =-\left(\frac{m-\frac{1}{2}}{1+\frac{m}{2}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\frac{m}{2}=-m+\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & m+\frac{m}{2}=\frac{1}{2}-1 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{3 m}{2}=\frac{-1}{2} \Rightarrow m=\frac{-1}{3} \end{array}$$

$\therefore$ First equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2=3(x-3) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 x-y-7=0 \end{aligned}$$

and second equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-2=-\frac{1}{3}(x-3) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 y-6=-x+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x+3 y-9=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The points $(3,4)$ and $(2,-6)$ are situated on the ............. of the line $3 x-4 y-8=0$.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{lc} \text { Given line is } & 3 x-4 y-8=0 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text { For point }(3,4), & 9-4 \cdot 4-8 \\ \Rightarrow & 9-16-8 \\ \Rightarrow & 9-24 \\ \Rightarrow & -15<0\quad \text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

For point $(2,-6)$,

$$\begin{aligned} 6 & +24-8 \\ 22 & >0\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Since, the value are of opposite sign.

Hence, the points $(3,4)$ and $(2,-6)$ lies on opposite side to the line.

en.content: A point moves so that square of its distance from the point $(3,-2)$ is numerically equal to its distance from the line $5 x-12 y=3$. The equation of its locus is ̣........... .
en.explanation:
Let the coordinaters of the point are $(h, k)$,

$\therefore \quad$ Distance between $(3,-2)$ and $(h, k)$,

$$d_1^2=(3-h)^2+(-2-k)^2\quad \text{... (i)}$$

Now, distance of the point $(h, k)$ from the line $5 x-12 y=3$ is,

$$d_2=\left|\frac{5 h-12 k-3}{\sqrt{25+144}}\right|=\left|\frac{5 h-12 k-3}{13}\right|\quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Given that, } \quad d_1^2=d_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(3-h)^2+(2+k)^2=\frac{5 h-12 k-3}{13} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 9-6 h+h^2+4+4 k+k^2=\frac{5 h-12 k-3}{13} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad h^2+k^2-6 h+4 k+13=\frac{5 h-12 k-3}{13} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 13 h^2+13 k^2-78 h+52 k+169=5 h-12 k-3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 13 h^2+13 k^2-83 h+64 k+172=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \text { Locus of this point is }\\ &13 x^2+13 y^2-83 x+64 y+172=0 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Locus of the mid-points of the portion of the line $x \sin \theta+y \cos \theta=p$ intercepted between the axes is $\ldots \ldots \ldots .$.
en.explanation:
Given equation of the line is

$$x \sin \theta+y \cos \theta=p\quad\text{... (i)}$$

Let the mid-point of $A B$ is $p(h, k)$.

So, the mid-point of $A B$ are $$\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2}\right)$$

Since, the point $(a, 0)$ lies on the line $(\mathrm{i})$, then

$$\begin{aligned} a \sin \theta+0 & =p \\ a \sin \theta & =p \Rightarrow a=\frac{p}{\sin \theta} \end{aligned}$$

and the point $(0, b)$ also lies on the line, then

$$\begin{aligned} & 0+b \cos \theta=p \\ & \Rightarrow \quad b \cos \theta=p \Rightarrow b=\frac{p}{\cos \theta} \\ & \text { Now, mid-point of } A B=\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2}\right) \text { or }\left(\frac{p}{2 \sin \theta}, \frac{p}{2 \cos \theta}\right) \\ & \because \quad \frac{p}{2 \sin \theta}=h \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{p}{2 h} \\ & \text { and } \quad \frac{p}{2 \cos \theta}=k \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{p}{2 k} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta=\frac{p^2}{4 h^2}+\frac{p^2}{4 k^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1=\frac{p^2}{4}\left(\frac{1}{h^2}+\frac{1}{k^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Locus of the mid-point is

$$4=p^2\left(\frac{1}{x^2}+\frac{1}{y^2}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 4 x^2 y^2=p^2\left(x^2+y^2\right)$$

en.content: If the vertices of a triangle have integral coordinates, then the triangle cannot be equilateral.
en.explanation:
We know that, if the vertices of a triangle have integral coordinates, then the triangle cannot be equilateral. Hence, the given statement is true.

Since, in equilateral triangle, we get $\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}=$ Slope of the line, so with integral coordinates as vertices, the triangle cannot be equilateral.

en.content: The points $A(-2,1), B(0,5)$ and $C(-1,2)$ are collinear.
en.explanation:
Given points are $A(-2,1), B(0,5)$ and $C(1,2)$.

Now, slope of $A B=\frac{5-1}{0+2}=2$

Slope of $$B C=\frac{2-5}{-1-0}=3$$

Slope of $$A C=\frac{2-1}{-1+2}=1$$

Since, the slopes are different.

Hence, $A, B$ and $C$ are not collinear. So, statement is false.

en.content: Equation of the line passing through the point $\left(a \cos ^3 \theta, a \sin ^3 \theta\right)$ and perpendicular to the line $x \sec \theta+y \operatorname{cosec} \theta=a$ is $x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a \sin 2 \theta$.
en.explanation:
Given point $p\left(a \cos ^3 \theta, a \sin ^3 \theta\right)$ and the line is $x \sec \theta+y \operatorname{cosec} \theta=a$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \because & \text { Slope of this line }=\frac{-\sec \theta}{\operatorname{cosec} \theta}=-\tan \theta \\ \text { and } & \text { slope of required line }=\frac{1}{\tan \theta}=\cot \theta \end{array}$$

$\therefore$ Equation of the required line is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & y-a \sin ^3 \theta=\cot \theta\left(x-a \cos ^3 \theta\right) \\ \Rightarrow & y \sin \theta-a \sin ^4 \theta=x \cos \theta-\operatorname{acos}^4 \theta \\ \Rightarrow & x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a \cos ^4 \theta-a \sin ^4 \theta \\ \Rightarrow & x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a\left[\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)\left(\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta\right)\right] \\ \Rightarrow & x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a \cos ^2 \theta \end{array}$$

Hence, the given statement is false.

en.content: The straight line $5 x+4 y=0$ passes through the point of intersection of the straight lines $x+2 y-10=0$ and $2 x+y+5=0$.
en.explanation:
Given that, $x+2 y-10=0\quad \text{.... (i)}$

and $2 x+y+5=0\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

From Eq. (i), put the value of $x=10-2 y$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{array}{rrr} \Rightarrow & 20-4 y+y+5 =0 \\ \Rightarrow & 20-3 y+5 =0 \\ \therefore & y+\frac{50}{3}-10 =0 \quad \text{[using Eq. (i)]}\\ \Rightarrow & x+\frac{20}{3}=0 \Rightarrow x =\frac{-20}{3} \end{array}$$

So, the point of intersection is $\left(-\frac{20}{3}, \frac{25}{3}\right)$.

If the line $5 x+4 y=0$ passes through the point $\left(-\frac{20}{3}, \frac{25}{3}\right)$, then this point should lie on this line.

$$5\left(\frac{-20}{3}\right)+\frac{4(25)}{3}=\frac{-100}{3}+\frac{100}{3}=0$$

So, this point lies on the given line.

Hence, the statement is true.

en.content: The vertex of an equilateral triangle is $(2,3)$ and the equation of the opposite side is $x+y=2$. Then, the other two sides are $y-3=(2 \pm \sqrt{3})(x-2)$.
en.explanation:
Let $A B C$ be an equilateral triangle with vertex $A(2,3)$, and equation of $B C$ is $x+y=2$. i.e., slope $=-1$.

Let slope of line $A B$ is $m$.

Since, the angle between line $A B$ and $B C$ is $60^{\circ}$.

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \therefore \tan 60 \Upsilon =\left|\frac{m+1}{1-m}\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3} = \pm\left(\frac{m+1}{1-m}\right) \quad \text{[taking positive sign]}\\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3} m =m+1 \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3}-1 =m+\sqrt{3} m \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3}-1 =m(1+\sqrt{3}) \\ & \therefore m =\frac{(\sqrt{3}-1)(\sqrt{3}-1)}{(\sqrt{3}+1)(\sqrt{3}-1)} \\ & =\frac{3+1-2 \sqrt{3}}{3-1}=\frac{4-2 \sqrt{3}}{2}=2-\sqrt{3} \end{array}$$

Similarly, slope of $A B=2+\sqrt{3}\quad$ [taking negative sign]

$\therefore$ Equation of other two side is

$$y-3=(2 \pm \sqrt{3})(x-2)$$

Hence, the statement is true.

en.content: The equation of the line joining the point $(3,5)$ to the point of intersection of the lines $4 x+y-1=0$ and $7 x-3 y-35=0$ is equidistant from the points $(0,0)$ and $(8,34)$.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{r} \text{Given equation of lines are}\quad 4 x+y-1=0 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text{and}\quad 7 x-3 y-35=0\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{array}$$

From Eq. (i), on putting $y=1-4 x$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{gathered} 7 x-3+12 x-35=0 \\ 19 x-38=0 \Rightarrow x=2 \end{gathered}$$

On putting $x=2$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$8+y-1=0 \Rightarrow y=-7$$

Now, the equation of a line passing through $(3,5)$ and $(2,-7)$ is

$$\begin{array}{l} & y-5 =\frac{-7-5}{2-3}(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & y-5 =12(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & 12 x-y-31 =0\quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{array}$$

Distance from $(0,0)$ to the line (iii),

$$d_1=\frac{|-31|}{\sqrt{144+1}}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{145}}$$

$\therefore$ Distance from $(8,34)$ to the line (iii),

$$\begin{aligned} & d_2=\frac{|96-34-31|}{\sqrt{145}}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{145}} \\ \because \quad & d_1=d_2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the statement is true.

en.content: The line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ moves in such a way that $\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{1}{c^2}$, where $c$ is a constant. The locus of the foot of the perpendicular from the origin on the given line is $x^2+y^2=c^2$.
en.explanation:
Given that, equation of line is

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1\quad \text{... (i)}$$

Equation of line passing through origin and perpendicular to line (i) is

$$\frac{x}{b}-\frac{y}{a}=0\quad \text{... (ii)}$$

Now, foot of perpendicular is the point of intersection of lines (i) and (ii). To find its locus we have to eliminate the variable $a$ and $b$.

On squaring and adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}+\frac{2 x y}{a b}+\frac{x^2}{b^2}+\frac{y^2}{a^2}-\frac{2 x y}{a b}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2\left(\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}\right)+y^2\left(\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}\right)=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^2}{c^2}+\frac{y^2}{c^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+y^2=c^2\quad \left[\because \frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{1}{c^2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the statement is true.

en.content: The lines $a x+2 y+1=0, \quad b x+3 y+1=0$ and $c x+4 y+1=0$ are concurrent, if $a, b$ and $c$ are in GP.
en.explanation:
Given lines are $a x+2 y+1=0$ ... (i)

and $b x+3 y+1=0$ ... (ii)

From Eq. (i), on putting $y=\frac{-a x-1}{2}$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & b x-\frac{3}{2}(a x+1)+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 b x-3 a x-3+2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x(2 b-3 a)=1 \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2 b-3 a} \end{array}$$

Now, using $x=\frac{1}{2 b-3 a}$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\frac{a}{2 b-3 a}+2 y+1=0$$

$\Rightarrow\quad 2 y=-\left[\frac{a+2 b-3 a}{2 b-3 a}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 2 y=\frac{-(2 b-2 a)}{2 b-3 a}$

$\Rightarrow y=\frac{(a-b)}{2 b-3 a}$

So, the point of intersection is $\left(\frac{1}{2 b-3 a}, \frac{a-b}{2 b-3 a}\right)$.

Since, this point lies on $c x+4 y+1=0$, then

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{c}{2 b-3 a}+\frac{4(a-b)}{2 b-3 a}+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & c+4 a-4 b+2 b-3 a=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -2 b+a+c=0 \Rightarrow 2 b=a+c \end{array}$$

Hence, the given statement is false.

en.content: Line joining the points $(3,-4)$ and $(-2,6)$ is perpendicular to the line joining the points $(-3,6)$ and $(9,-18)$.
en.explanation:
Given points are $A(3,-4), B(-2,6), P(-3,6)$ and $Q(9,-18)$.

Now,

slope of $A B=\frac{6+4}{-2-3}=-2$

and slope of $P Q=\frac{-18-6}{9+3}=-2$

So, line $A B$ is parallel to line $P Q$.

en.content:
Match the following.

Column I Column II
(i) The coordinates of the points $P$ and $Q$ on the line $x+5 y=13$ which are at a distance of 2 units from the line $12 x-5 y+26=0$ are (a) $(3,1),(-7,11)$
(ii) The coordinates of the point on the line $x+y=4$, which are at a unit distance from the line $4 x+3 y-10=0$ are (b) $\left(-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{11}{3}\right),\left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right)$
(iii) The coordinates of the point on the line joining $A(-2,5)$ and $B(3,1)$ such that $A P=P Q=Q B$ are (c) $\left(1, \frac{12}{5}\right),\left(-3, \frac{16}{5}\right)$
en.explanation:
(i) Let the coordinate of point $P\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ on the line $x+5 y=13 i$.e.,

$$P\left(13-5 y_1, y_1\right)$$

$\therefore$ Distance of $P$ from the line $12 x-5 y+26=0$,

$$\begin{array}{l} & 2 =\left|\frac{12\left(13-5 y_1\right)-5 y_1+26}{\sqrt{144+25}}\right| \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 2 = \pm \frac{156-60 y_1-5 y_1+26}{13} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & -65 y_1 =-156 \quad \text{[taking positive sign]}\\ \Rightarrow \quad& y_1 =\frac{156}{65}=\frac{12}{5} \\ \because \quad & x_1=13-5 y_1 \\ & =13-12=1 \end{array}$$

So, the coordinate of is $P\left(1, \frac{12}{5}\right)$.

Similarly, the coordinates of $Q$ are $\left(-3, \frac{16}{5}\right)\quad \text{[taking negative sign]}$.

(ii) Let coordinates of the point on the line $x+y=4$ be $\left(4-y_1, y_1\right)$.

Distance from the line $4 x+3 y-10=0$.

$$\begin{aligned} & 1=\left|\frac{4\left(4-y_1\right)+3 y_1-10}{\sqrt{16+9}}\right| \\ \Rightarrow & 1= \pm \frac{16-4 y_1+3 y_1-10}{5} \quad\text{[taking negative sign]}\\ \Rightarrow & 5=6-y_1 \\ \Rightarrow & y_1=1 \end{aligned}$$

If $y_1=1$, then $x_1=3$

So, the point is $(3,1)$.

Similarly, taking negative sign the point is $(-7,11)$.

(iii) Given point $A(-2,5)$ and $B(3,1)$.

Now, the point $P$ divides line joining the point $A$ and $B$ in $1: 2$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \because & x_1=\frac{1 \cdot 3+2(-2)}{1+2}=\frac{3-4}{3}=\frac{-1}{3} \\ \text { and } & y_1=\frac{1 \cdot 1+2 \cdot 5}{1+2}=\frac{11}{3} \end{array}$$

So, the coordinates of $P$ are $\left(\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{11}{3}\right)$.

Thus, the point $Q$ divided the line joining $A$ to $B$ in $2: 1$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \because & x_2=\frac{2 \cdot 3+1(-2)}{2+1}=\frac{4}{3} \\ \text { and } & y_2=\frac{2 \cdot 1+1 \cdot 5}{2+1}=\frac{7}{3} \end{array}$$

Hence, the coordinates of $Q$ are $\left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right)$.

Hence, the correct matches are (i) $\rightarrow$ (c), (ii) $\rightarrow$ (a), (iii) $\rightarrow$ (b).

en.content:
The value of the $\lambda$, if the lines $(2+3 y+4)+\lambda(6 x-y+12)=0$ are

Column I Column II
(i) parallel to Y-axis is (a) $\lambda=-\frac{3}{4}$
(ii) perpendicular to $7x+y-4=0$ is (b) $\lambda=-\frac{1}{3}$
(iii) passes through $(1,2)$ is (c)` $\lambda=-\frac{17}{41}$
(iv) parallel to $x$-axis is (d) $\lambda=3$
en.explanation:
(i) Given equation of the line is

$$(2 x+3 y+4)+\lambda(6 x-y+12)=0\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

If line is parallel to $Y$-axis i.e., it is perpendicular to $X$-axis

$$\therefore \quad \text { Slope }=m=\tan 90 \Upsilon=\infty$$

From line (i), $x(2+6 \lambda)+y(3-\lambda)+4+12 \lambda=0$

and slope $$=\frac{-(2+6 \lambda)}{3-\lambda}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{-2-6 \lambda}{3-\lambda}=\infty \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{-2-6 \lambda}{3-\lambda}=\frac{1}{0} \Rightarrow \lambda=3 \end{array}$$

(ii) If the line (i) is perpendicular to the line $7 x+y-4=0$ or $y=-7 x+4$

$$\begin{aligned} \because \quad & \frac{-(2+6 \lambda)}{(3-\lambda)}(-7) =-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 14+42 \lambda =-3+\lambda \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 41 \lambda =-17 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \lambda =-\frac{17}{41} \end{aligned}$$

(iii) If the line (i) passes through the point $(1,2)$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Then, }\quad (2+6+4)+\lambda(6-2+12) & =0 \\ 12+16 \lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda & =-\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

(iv) If the line is parallel to $X$-axis the slope $=0$.

Then, $$\frac{-(2+6 \lambda)}{3-\lambda}=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad-(2+6 \lambda)=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{3}$$

So, the correct matches are (i) $\rightarrow$ (d), (ii) $\rightarrow$ (c), (iii) $\rightarrow$ (a), (iv) $\rightarrow$ (b).

en.content:
The equation of the line through the intersection of the lines $2 x-3 y=0$ and $4 x-5 y=2$ and

Column I Column II
(i) through the point $(2,1)$ is (a) $2x-y=4$
(ii) perpendicular to the line $x+2y+1=0$ is (b) $x+y-5=0$
(iii) parallel to the line $3x-4y+5=0$ is (c)` $x-y-1=0$
(iv) equally inclined to the axes is (d) $3x-4y-1=0$
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given equation of the lines are}\quad & 2 x-3 y=0 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ \text{and}\quad & 4 x-5 y=2\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eq. (i), put $x=\frac{3 y}{2}$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 4 \cdot \frac{(3 y)}{2}-5 y & =2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 6 y-5 y & =2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad y & =2 \end{aligned}$$

Now, put $y=2$ in Eq. (i), we get $$x=3$$

So, the intersection points are $(3,2)$.

(i) The equation of the line passes through the point $(3,2)$ and $(2,1)$, is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & y-2 & =\frac{1-2}{2-3}(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & y-2 & =(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & x-y-1 & =0 \end{array}$$

(ii) If the required line is perpendicular to the line $x+2 y+1=0$

$\because$ Slope of the required line $=2$

$\therefore$ Equation of the line is

$$y-2=2(x-3)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x-y-4=0$$

(iii) If the required line is parallel to the line $3 x-4 y+5=0$, then the slope of the required line $=\frac{3}{4}$

$\therefore$ Equation of the required line is

$$y-2=\frac{3}{4}(x-3)$$

$$\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow & 4 y-8=3 x-9 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x-4 y-1=0 \end{array}$$

(iv) If the line is equally inclined to the $X$-axis, then

$$m= \pm \tan 45 \Upsilon= \pm 1$$

$\therefore$ Equation of the line is

$$\begin{array}{l} & y-2 & =-1(x-3) \text{[taking negative value]}\\ \Rightarrow & y-2 & =-x+3 \\ \Rightarrow & x+y-5 & =0 \end{array}$$

So, the correct matches are (a) $\rightarrow$ (iii), (b) $\rightarrow$ (i), (c) $\rightarrow$ (iv), (d) $\rightarrow$ (ii).
